item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first two images had no indication of being released under Creative Commons (here and here), and the third image could not be reliably sourced. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would be better if you had chosen coords for an actual military training ground, but +1 for effort

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wardogs in ww1 being displaced in time and space when they are hurt? Have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was gonna go all neutral vote on this one at first. I thought it was good, but not quite good enough to try and counter the low rating. Then, after I went back to the big list and saw that this article was titled "Mercy in the Storm", the last bit of the full picture became clear to me and an upvote was earned.

+1

item.title: Nerd Nit-Pick
item.content:encoded:
A post-mortem on a dog (or any non-human animal) isn't called an Autopsy but a Necropsy. The term Autopsy uses the Latin root "Auto", which means "to do to ones self", in this case one human examining the remains of another.

Unless the coroner is a dog, which would also be cool. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found this one via Random SCP.

When I saw the picture, I was expecting a duckhunt reference, lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a couple grammar errors and stylistic bumps, like not capitalizing respiratory inflammation. And 'no differentiation from'.

Perhaps you could mention that the dogs are of breeds common at WWI, or not, if they're not. I don't know much about dog breeds.

I don't like the fact that the dogs rapidly decompose. It makes the SCP seem a little slim if nothing persists. Maybe have the dogs carrying tools, messages, or booze (painkiller) like I think WWI rescue dogs did?

In regards to the containment procedures, a 30m military training ground is suspiciously small. People would wonder why a chunk of land the size of a house was barb-wired off, instead of a whole field. You need to make it a kilometer or something and build a shooting range to keep the Foundation's covertness strong.

Oh, and redacting only half of a percentage is very annoying.

Downvoted for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like it but I'll have to echo the sentiment that the various elements are too random to really give this SCP a proper… identity. That's the hardest thing about writing SCP entries: they have to be weird, but still have some sort of logic behind them because if it's just random stuff happening, you don't really have much material to speculate on what's going on. You have to give the impression that there is a reason behind the SCP, even if we can't comprehend it.

On a more specific level, I am very confused as to why, in the last paragraph, they would only block out the first number of whatever the percentage is. The person writing this report would either block the whole thing or none of it, wouldn't they? It just looks kind of silly that way (and frankly I'm not sure the 1652-1 incident is necessary to begin with).

All in all, interesting concept but needs to be better fleshed out. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This left me with a deep "So what?" feeling. I see what's going on, I just don't know why I should care about any of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During a period of four hours each day, gunshots and explosions will be heard within a 15 meter radius of SCP-1652.

Which four hours each day? Is it same hours every day, or does it shift over time, or is it completely random?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dogs were part of the anomaly. It WAS Safe until an actual person came out of it, which meant they need to revise how they treat the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole thing is a little too random for me. For example, why only dogs? I mean, there would have to be a whole lot more people than dogs on a WWI battlefield. It just seems a bit random that this field is spitting out dogs from a WWI battlefield, especially ones that seem happy to return to the batttlefield.

Also, why is this Euclid? It doesn't seem like it harms anybody; it just produces things that were already harmed.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first image is linked directly from this site, so I'm not sure why it's broken, unless it was removed from the visual records. Can you explain how to store the other two images locally? I'll fix them as soon as I'm not on mobile.

Edit: Looked at the first image and it's working. Nothing seems to be wrong with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you wouldn't mind, might you upload your files locally? The first image appears to be broken and the second and third are hosted off-site, which they probably shouldn't be IIRC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea why this is happening. As far as I can tell it's a semi-random collection of ideas. I think there's a story somewhere in here, but it's impossible for me to find. The effects and concept don't make up for this. Am I missing something? I mean, it seems like there's something more here, but I'm unsure what, exactly, it is.
Downvote, because I want to see this improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding Roget right here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I discussed with others quite a bit on blocking things out. Blocking out the amount of years was for a shock factor, and so was the corpse of SCP-1652-2. It is to give your mind a bit more to work with. It could be anywhere from 10 to 99 years of decomposition with SCP-1652-1. Either way, if you think about it, neither one is very pleasant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seemed like some things were blocked out just to be blocked out. No vote yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't quite get what was going on, but I still like this for being so darn weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spaced the paragraphs out. If this goes negative, I'm retracting it and adding more connections with a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Come on, I already told you to space those paragraphs out. >:I
I'm still not seeing what's supposed to be happening here, other than a perpetual WWI battlefield. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Vaguely interesting, but not much more than that. Space those paragraphs out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On a side note. I changed the name.

I looked up battles, and dogs were used as guards and message transport during WWI, and were indeed used in France. It being in Tennessee is part of the anomaly, because it is not normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can you elaborate on what you mean by random elements?

Well, for starters, why the hell is this thing in Tennessee? Seems an anomalous WWI battlefield would actually be where WWI occurred, unless there's a reason it isn't, which isn't given in the article.

Why dogs? I mean, I can think of narrative reasons to do so (though I don't necessarily agree with said reasons), but why in the story does this happen? And then it's actually humans too at the end, which undermines any internal consistency this may have had.

You're using some powerful imagery (at least to me), and from my standpoint, it's not amounting to anything more than "well here's some stuff that's happening for some reason".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually came up with the title last minute. Can you elaborate on what you mean by random elements?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all seriousness, I didn't downvote based strictly on the title, although it's corny and makes me suspect that you started with the title and wrote something to justify it. I downvoted based on a mix of issues with the article (there's too many random elements for a cohesive narrative) and my own personal tastes when it comes to WWI stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad it's not the standard "scip what makes other scips" article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a reason why "Dogs of war" caused you to downvote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*reads skip*

*thinks to self, "please don't be called 'dogs of war', please"*

*checks mainlist*

*downvotes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Dog in the Rye" Belongs solely to reddit user Lacostic with his permission.
